ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 covers lime juice as a category; no specific entry for home-made preparation exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers lime juice generically; home-made variant not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229958007 covers lime juice as a substance; home-made preparation not separately coded. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass of home-made lime juice, approximately 240 ml, as typically served in a Filipino café or home setting.

nutrient_source.title: Lime juice, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167752)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Home-made lime juice is modelled on USDA SR Legacy entry for raw lime juice (FDC ID 167752), which represents freshly squeezed lime juice without added sugar. Values are per 100 g of the juice as squeezed. If sugar is added in the home-made version, energy and carbohydrate values would be higher; values here represent the unsweetened baseline.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for home-made lime juice. GI of 20 is estimated by analogy with unsweetened citrus juices (lemon juice, grapefruit juice) which are consistently reported in the low GI range (GI 20–25) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (20 × 20.2) / 100 ≈ 4.0, based on 240 g serving with 8.42 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (2 mg per 100 g) and moderate potassium (117 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Home-made lime juice is very low in sodium and provides potassium, making it a suitable beverage for individuals managing hypertension, provided no excessive salt or sugar is added during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~20) and modest carbohydrate content (8.42 g per 100 g); sugar content increases if sweetened
type2_diabetes.guidance: Unsweetened home-made lime juice has a low GI and modest carbohydrate load, making it acceptable in moderate portions for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; sweetened versions should be avoided or strictly portioned to limit glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and vitamin C content supporting vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Home-made lime juice is essentially fat-free and cholesterol-free, and its vitamin C content (30 mg per 100 g) may support endothelial function, making it a heart-friendly beverage choice when consumed without added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Home-made lime juice is a freshly squeezed beverage prepared by pressing or hand-squeezing fresh limes and straining the juice, typically served chilled or over ice. In Filipino café culture, it is a popular refreshment offered alongside meals or as a standalone drink, often lightly sweetened with sugar or syrup to balance the tartness of the lime. The juice has a bright, sharp citrus flavour with a pronounced acidity and a clean, refreshing finish. Nutritionally, unsweetened lime juice provides approximately 25 kcal per 100 g, with 8.42 g of carbohydrate, 0.42 g of protein, and only 0.07 g of fat. It is a meaningful source of vitamin C (30 mg per 100 g), contributing to immune function and collagen synthesis. Sodium content is very low at 2 mg per 100 g, and potassium is present at 117 mg per 100 g, supporting cardiovascular and renal health. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 20 (low), making it suitable for individuals monitoring blood glucose when consumed unsweetened. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes (unsweetened), and cardiovascular disease. The beverage is commonly served in Filipino restaurants and cafés in Singapore, including casual dining establishments.
common_user_queries: Is lime juice good for diabetics?
How many calories in fresh lime juice?
What is the GI of lime juice?
Is lime juice low in sodium?
Can I drink lime juice if I have high blood pressure?
How much vitamin C is in lime juice?
What is the carbohydrate content of lime juice?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw lime juice data (FDC ID 167752), which is a well-validated primary source for freshly squeezed lime juice. The match is adapted rather than exact because the home-made preparation may include added sugar; values represent the unsweetened baseline. GI is an analogous estimate from approved sources. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted source match and the variability introduced by optional sweetening.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are based on USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 167752 (lime juice, raw) representing unsweetened freshly squeezed lime juice per 100 g. If sugar is added, energy_kcal, carbohydrate_g, and sugar_g will be higher than reported here. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA source; naturally zero in a plant-derived fruit juice). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero in a plant-derived beverage). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero as this is a plant-derived food with no animal-derived components). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero; lime juice contains no vitamin D). 3. glycemic_index is set to 20 as an analogous estimate for unsweetened citrus juice based on the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008; gi_category is low. glycemic_load_per_serving is 4.0 based on a 240 g serving. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are reported from USDA SR Legacy. 5. The food is listed under Filipino cuisine as served at Lucky Cafe - North Canal Road (field survey data), though lime juice is a broadly Southeast Asian beverage. 6. Multilingual names follow the Singaporean dishes rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this food is served in Singapore. 7. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no exact home-made lime juice entry exists in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT. 8. All -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with actual USDA-reported values where available; no -1 sentinel values remain in this record.
